hq: Solar House, 282 Chase Road, London, England, N14 6NZ

system_description.overview: hyperGTM is an AI-powered go-to-market platform built on AI agents that help revenue teams connect with hard-to-reach prospects. The agents design and deliver alternative, highly qualified outreach campaigns enabling personalized, experiential engagement with decision-makers through in-person events and direct mail.

products.Account-Based Campaign Management Platfo: AI agents orchestrating targeted 1:1 GTM campaigns with multi-channel engagement across email, social, direct-mail, and experiential campaigns with real-time analytics
products.Campaign Management Tools: Centralized design, launch, and tracking of outreach campaigns with workflow automation, customizable templates, and performance insights
products.Intelligent Prospect Targeting: AI-driven identification of high-value accounts and stakeholders with data-enriched insights and adaptive targeting models

regions: Not explicitly disclosed; Vercel multi-AZ mentioned
infrastructure.physical_presence: None - no physical offices or servers; all cloud-based
infrastructure.availability_zones: Multi-AZ (via Vercel high availability data centers with multiple availability zones)
infrastructure.cdn: Vercel Edge Network CDN + Stric Assets CDN (via Vercel Blob)

network_architecture.network_segmentation: Hosted in Vercel VPC environment with segmented VPCs and access control lists (ACLs)
network_architecture.api_gateway: Vercel Edge Network (acts as application gateway per network diagram)
network_architecture.waf: WAF present in network diagram between CDN and Edge Network
network_architecture.firewalls: System firewalls configured on application gateway and production network to limit unnecessary ports, protocols, and services. Firewall rules reviewed annually by IT management (CC6.6.1). Formal network diagram outlining boundary protection mechanisms including firewalls and IDS maintained (CC2.3.4).
network_architecture.ids_ips: IDS and IPS via Vercel to identify and protect against threats in conjunction with security policy network settings
network_architecture.vpn: Remote access via bastion host with VPN
network_architecture.tls: TLS encryption for all user requests using certificates from established third-party certificate authority
network_architecture.content_filtering: Content filtering system routed through proxy server; all systems restricted to internet through this
network_architecture.topology: Internet -> User Browser -> HTTPS -> CDN -> WAF -> Vercel Edge Network -> Next.js Functions -> (Auth Service, Auth Provider, Neon DB & Realtime, AI APIs [Gemini/OpenAI/Perplexity], Communication Services [Email/Social Media], Background Jobs [Inngest Cloud]). GCP Backup via scheduled cron to GCS Bucket. Static assets served via Vercel Blob -> Stric Assets CDN.

application_architecture.api_style: REST (HTTPS connections visible in network diagram)
application_architecture.environments: Separate development/test and production environments (CC8.1.4 confirms changes developed and tested in separate environment before implementation; Stage and Production mentioned)

databases: Neon (PostgreSQL - serverless Postgres, visible in network diagram as 'Neon/DB & Realtime')
data_storage.backup_frequency: Scheduled exports via cron to GCS Bucket (frequency not specified beyond 'suitable backups are taken')

authentication_access_control.identity_provider: Auth Provider visible in network diagram (specific vendor not named); Vercel password policy for internal access
authentication_access_control.mfa: MFA enforced for user accounts with administrative access to production platform (CC6.1.1); MFA on source code management tool (CC8.1.3); MFA required for email access
authentication_access_control.access_revocation_sla: Within one business day upon termination of employment (CC6.1.4)
authentication_access_control.password_policy: Controlled through Vercel password policy including periodic forced changes, password expiry, complexity requirements. Accounts disabled after limited unsuccessful logon attempts. Guest and anonymous logins not allowed. Desktops locked after inactivity.

encryption.at_rest: Cloud provider key management service used to encrypt data at rest (CC6.1.5)
encryption.key_management: Cloud provider key management service (CC6.1.5); access to production environment access keys restricted to authorized individuals

ci_cd_devops.source_control: Source code management tool (not named explicitly; likely GitHub based on MFA requirement and branch protection)
ci_cd_devops.ci_cd_tool: Not explicitly named; formal change management process with internal tool tracking from initiation through deployment and validation
ci_cd_devops.branch_protection: Code repository branch rules configured to ensure every merge request to move changes to production requires additional approval (CC8.1.2)
ci_cd_devops.iac: Baseline configurations retained as infrastructure as code; configuration management tool used for implementation and rollback (CC7.1.1)
ci_cd_devops.patch_management: Automatic patch management with monthly reviews by CTO. OS patches marked critical/security applied as they become available. Workstations configured for automatic updates and scanned for patch compliance daily (CC7.5.1).

monitoring_logging.apm_tool: Not named; monitoring tools used for continuous system capacity and performance monitoring (CC5.2.1)
monitoring_logging.log_aggregation: Logging enabled to monitor administrative activities, logon attempts, data deletions at application and infrastructure level, security configurations, permissions, and roles (CC7.2.1)
monitoring_logging.alerting: Automated alerts configured to notify IT management of high and critical risk events; predefined threshold-based alerting (CC5.2.1, CC7.2.1)

third_party_services.purpose: Primary cloud hosting, CDN, edge network, serverless compute, VPC infrastructure
Serverless PostgreSQL database and realtime data layer
Backup storage (GCS Bucket for scheduled database exports)
Email delivery for multi-channel outreach campaigns
Social media integration for campaign engagement
Static asset delivery via Vercel Blob

security_tools.antivirus: Anti-virus and Malware protection installed on all production servers (continuous scanning), Mac workstations and laptops. Anti-virus definitions updated weekly for production servers, daily for workstations. Email scanning included (CC6.8.1).
security_tools.vulnerability_scanning: Monthly automated vulnerability scans on infrastructure and applications, with frequency adjusted as required. Self-assessment vulnerability scan prior to every production release or yearly (CC7.1.2).
security_tools.penetration_testing: Annual external penetration testing by independent third-party. High/critical findings remediated and tracked via incident management (CC4.1.1).

excluded_criteria: CC6.4 - Physical access controls excluded; Vercel responsible for physical security of data centers
A1.2 - Environmental protections excluded; hyperGTM has no office/manages from working space managed by building management; environmental security managed by building management / Vercel
Processing Integrity - Not relevant to the system
Privacy - Not relevant to the system
notable_controls: CC8.1.2: Branch protection enforced - developers cannot make changes to production without additional approval via merge request
CC8.1.3: Source code changes logged, time-stamped, attributed to author with MFA-protected access to source code tool
CC6.1.3: Per-tenant data segregation - system configured so customer access is appropriately segmented from other customer accounts
CC6.6.1: Firewalls and intrusion detection tools configured on application gateway; firewall rules reviewed annually
CC7.4.1: BCDR policy documented and reviewed annually with testing per defined schedule for different loss scenarios
CC9.2.1: Vendor management policy with annual SOC report reviews and quarterly risk assessments for critical vendors without SOC reports
CC4.1.1: Annual external penetration testing by independent third-party

infrastructure.rationale: Vercel provides solid serverless infrastructure with multi-AZ and CDN. However, single cloud provider (Vercel) with carve-out means heavy dependency. No multi-region explicitly confirmed. GCP used only for backups. Edge network and WAF are good. Regions not explicitly disclosed.

application_architecture.rationale: Modern serverless Next.js architecture on Vercel is appropriate for their scale. Branch protection and code review enforced. Separate environments confirmed. However, only one language/framework mentioned, no mention of testing frameworks, and limited detail on service decomposition. API style is basic REST.

data_layer.rationale: Neon serverless Postgres is modern choice. GCS backup exists. Data classification and retention policies in place. Per-tenant segregation confirmed. However, no caching layer mentioned, no data warehouse, backup frequency not specified, and single database type for all needs.

security_posture.rationale: Strong security fundamentals: WAF, IDS/IPS, MFA on admin/source code, annual pen testing, monthly vulnerability scanning, endpoint protection, anti-virus, server hardening, VPN for remote access, encryption at rest and in transit, access reviews quarterly. Formal incident response process. Good for a small company. Missing: specific SIEM, named monitoring tools, TLS version not specified.

devops_maturity.rationale: IaC with configuration management and rollback. Branch protection with merge request approval. Source code management with MFA. Formal change management with emergency change process. Separate environments. Monthly patch management reviews. However, CI/CD tool not named, no container orchestration (serverless mitigates this), and limited automation detail.

bcdr_readiness.rationale: BCDR policy exists, documented, and reviewed annually. Testing conducted annually for different loss scenarios. Multi-AZ failover via Vercel. GCS backup exports. However, RTO/RPO not disclosed, no multi-region failover, backup frequency unclear, and DR plan relies heavily on Vercel's carve-out infrastructure.

vendor_diversity.rationale: Good diversity with 8+ vendors visible in architecture: Vercel (infra), Neon (DB), three AI providers (OpenAI, Gemini, Perplexity), Inngest (jobs), GCP (backup), plus email and social providers. Multiple AI providers reduce single-vendor AI risk. However, critical infrastructure dependency on Vercel as single cloud provider is a concentration risk.

overall.rationale: hyperGTM demonstrates solid security fundamentals for an early-stage startup with a small team (CEO, CTO, CPO, Founding Engineer). Modern serverless architecture on Vercel is appropriate. Strong vendor management and formal compliance processes. Key gaps are lack of specificity around backup/DR targets (RTO/RPO), single-provider infrastructure dependency on Vercel, and several named tools/versions not disclosed. The SOC 2 Type 1 (point-in-time) rather than Type 2 (observation period) limits assurance on operational effectiveness.

red_flags: Very small team (4 people: CEO, CTO, CPO, Founding Engineer) - key-person risk is extreme; loss of CTO or Founding Engineer could cripple operations
SOC 2 Type 1 only (point-in-time, no observation period) - controls are designed but not yet proven to operate effectively over time
Vercel as sole infrastructure provider with carve-out method - zero control visibility into critical subservice organization
RTO and RPO not disclosed despite having BCDR policy
No multi-region failover - single region failure at Vercel could cause total outage
Signing authority designation field is blank - unusual for a formal compliance document
yellow_flags: Many security tools not named (SIEM, APM, monitoring tool, CI/CD tool, source code management) - makes independent verification difficult
TLS version not specified - could be running older TLS versions
Backup frequency not explicitly stated - only 'suitable backups are taken'
No mention of SSO for customer access
Processing Integrity and Privacy criteria excluded as 'not relevant' despite handling customer prospect data and AI-driven targeting
Password policy controlled through Vercel - raises questions about internal application authentication controls
Auditor firm (Accorp Partners) is not a well-known Big 4 or mid-tier firm
green_flags: Modern serverless architecture (Next.js on Vercel) is cost-efficient and scales well for a startup
Three AI providers (OpenAI, Gemini, Perplexity) show smart vendor diversification for core AI capabilities
MFA enforced at multiple levels: production admin, source code, email access
Branch protection with mandatory merge request approval prevents unauthorized production changes
Annual penetration testing by independent third-party
Monthly vulnerability scanning with automated tooling
Quarterly user access reviews including privileged accounts
Formal vendor management with annual SOC report reviews and quarterly assessments for vendors without SOC
BCDR plan tested annually across different loss scenarios
GCP used for backup storage provides infrastructure diversity for critical data protection
IaC with configuration management and rollback capability
key_observations: Network diagram reveals a much richer architecture than the text describes - three AI API providers (Gemini, OpenAI, Perplexity) and Neon database are only visible in the diagram, not mentioned in the system description text
The company is UK-based (London) but appears to operate entirely on US-based cloud infrastructure (Vercel, Neon, GCP) - potential GDPR/data residency considerations
For a 4-person startup, the control framework is surprisingly comprehensive, suggesting use of a compliance automation platform (e.g., Vanta, Drata) though none is named

The carve-out of Vercel is strategically important - much of hyperGTM's security posture (IDS/IPS, VPC, physical security, multi-AZ) is actually Vercel's responsibility, not hyperGTM's direct implementation
Inngest Cloud for background jobs suggests event-driven architecture patterns, which aligns well with AI agent orchestration use case
The Stric Assets CDN appearing in the diagram suggests use of Vercel Blob storage for static assets, indicating the application serves both dynamic AI-generated content and static marketing assets

Report uses heavily templated language (especially in control descriptions with many repeated CC1.4.1 and CC2.3.3 controls across multiple criteria), typical of compliance automation platforms generating SOC 2 reports
